planting.note: Plant container-grown trees or large cuttings in spring in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; june plum is very fast-growing and can begin fruiting from cuttings in 2–3 years. Hardy to about 30°F; best in zone 9b–12.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; june plum (ambarella) is a fast-growing tropical fruit tree of the Pacific Islands and Southeast Asia, producing clusters of oval green-to-yellow fruits 2–3 inches long that are consumed at all stages of ripeness. Unripe fruits are prized throughout Asia and the Caribbean for their crisp, tart flesh, used in fresh salads, pickled condiments, and green mango-style preparations; ripe fruits are sweet-tart with a fibrous spiny seed and are eaten fresh or juiced. The tree is extremely fast-growing (10–15 feet in the first 2 years), the leaves are edible as salad greens when young, and the tree propagates easily from large hardwood cuttings that fruit in 2–3 years. It thrives in zone 9b–12 as a reliable, low-maintenance, highly productive tropical fruit tree and is one of the best 'beginner' tropical fruits for warm-climate gardens.

planting.note: Plant large hardwood cuttings directly in full sun in any well-drained soil; red mombin roots and establishes extremely easily from 1–2 foot branch cuttings inserted directly into the ground. Established trees tolerate brief freezes to 28°F; best in zone 9b–12.

directions: Plant large hardwood cuttings directly in well-drained soil in full sun — red mombin is a small tropical plum tree of Central America and the Caribbean notable for two things: the flavor of its egg-shaped, wine-red to dark purple fruits (a thin skin over sweet-tart orange flesh with an enormous seed, eaten fresh or in agua fresca throughout Mexico and Central America where it is called 'jocote') and its ability to propagate from large cuttings stuck directly in the ground — even fenceposts made from the wood can sprout. The deciduous tree drops its leaves in the dry season, then blooms and leafs out simultaneously in the rainy season. It is remarkably drought tolerant and is grown in poor, dry soils throughout the tropics. The small reddish fruits ripen over a long season and are consumed by birds and wildlife. An excellent low-water, easy-care tropical fruit tree for zone 9b–12.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; grumichama tolerates brief temperatures to 28–30°F once established, making it suitable for zone 9b–12. The plant grows slowly but is long-lived and very ornamental.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade — grumichama is a beautiful small-to-medium Brazilian Eugenia producing glossy, deep burgundy-to-black cherries (3/4 inch) with sweet, soft, slightly acidic flesh and 1–3 seeds, eaten fresh, made into preserves, or used in beverages. The trees are ornamental year-round with attractive smooth, pinkish-brown peeling bark, handsome dark glossy leaves, and masses of white flowers before fruiting. Unlike many tropical fruits it tolerates partial shade and moist conditions and has some cold hardiness to about 28°F. The fruits are produced in late spring and are consumed enthusiastically by birds. Closely related to jaboticaba and surinam cherry, the grumichama is one of the finest but least known of the Brazilian Eugenia fruits, producing a fruit that resembles a cherry in size and sweetness.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun; mountain soursop grows and fruits faster than common soursop (A. muricata) and shows slightly more cold tolerance, performing in zone 9b–12.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil in full sun — mountain soursop is the larger-fruited relative of common soursop (Annona muricata), native to the rainforests of the Amazon basin, and is considered more vigorous and faster-fruiting than its more common relative. The spiny, ovoid fruits (3–6 inches) have white, fibrous, juicy flesh with a strong tropical flavor used to make juice, ice cream, and desserts similar to soursop. The tree is faster-growing than common soursop and shows slightly greater cold tolerance. Like all Annona species, the flowers produce heat and attract beetle pollinators overnight — cross-pollination with another tree improves fruit set significantly. The handsome, large, dark-green glossy leaves make it an attractive ornamental even when not in fruit. A productive, fast-bearing tropical fruit tree for zone 9b–12 gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; bignay is dioecious — both male and female trees are needed for fruit; female trees produce large, pendulous clusters of berries. Hardy to about 28°F in zone 9b–12.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — bignay is a medium-sized tropical tree of Southeast Asia and the Pacific, producing spectacular long (12–18 inch) pendulous clusters of small (pea-sized) berries that ripen from green through red to deep purple-black in sequence on the same cluster, creating a striking multi-colored effect. The berries have a pleasant tart-sweet flavor with some astringency and are eaten fresh, made into wine, juice, and jam; in the Philippines bignay wine is a traditional fermented beverage. The tree is dioecious and shade-tolerant, growing naturally as an understory tree in mixed tropical forests. The polished, reddish-brown seeds (resembling small nutmegs) are made into decorative necklaces in the Philippines. An outstanding ornamental fruiting tree for zone 9b–12 gardens offering shade tolerance and dramatic fruiting clusters.
